title: Memory Networks
authors: Jason Weston, Sumit Chopra, Antoine Bordes
hook: Imagine a neural network that can look up facts before answering your question.
promise: You will understand how memory networks let AI retrieve and reason over stored knowledge for question answering.
prerequisite_note: Bring an intuition for how neural networks process information and why memory might help them reason.

glossary.plain: A part of the model where information can be stored, updated, and retrieved when needed.
The reasoning part that decides which facts from memory are relevant to answer a question.
A task where a system receives a question and must generate or select the correct answer.
The process of connecting multiple pieces of information to reach a conclusion.
A flexible, updatable set of facts the model can use, rather than a fixed database.

cards.title: Let AI Remember
body: Discover how giving neural networks memory lets them answer questions by retrieving and reasoning over facts.

cards.title: Why can't neural nets remember?
body: Standard neural networks are great at pattern recognition but struggle to recall specific facts or details from past inputs.
Imagine asking a chatbot about your last vacation. Without memory, it cannot connect your question to any stored details, so its answers are generic.

cards.title: How memory networks work
body: First, the model stores sentences or facts in a dedicated memory component, like writing notes on a whiteboard.
When a question arrives, the inference component decides which stored facts are relevant, retrieves them, and reasons over the results to produce an answer.
cards.middle: Retrieve and reason over relevant facts
cards.caption: Memory networks separate storage and reasoning, allowing questions to trigger targeted retrieval from memory.

cards.title: How does memory change QA?
body: Switch between answering questions without memory and with memory networks to see the difference.
cards.offNote: Answers are vague or miss key details, since the model cannot recall specific facts.
cards.onNote: Model retrieves and chains relevant facts, giving more precise and context-aware answers.

cards.title: A neural net with a notebook
body: Memory networks treat knowledge as something the model can read and write, not just compress into weights.
This lets the model chain together multiple facts, retrieving just what it needs to answer complex questions.

cards.title: How much memory is enough?
body: Slide to see how the size of memory affects the model's ability to answer questions.
cards.low: Model quickly forgets details and cannot answer multi-step questions.
cards.mid: Model remembers enough to answer simple questions but struggles with deeper reasoning.
cards.high: Model can chain many facts and reason through complex scenarios, but retrieval is slower.

cards.title: What goes into a memory network?
body: Pick the components needed to construct a functioning memory network model.

choices.label: Memory slots
choices.note: Hold the facts or sentences to be stored.

choices.label: Inference module
choices.note: Selects which memories are relevant to a question.

choices.label: Input feature map
choices.note: Encodes the incoming question and facts into a usable form.

choices.label: Output feature map
choices.note: Converts the combined result back into an answer.

choices.label: Memory update rule
choices.note: Decides how new information is added or old facts are changed.

choices.label: Supervision signal
choices.note: Guides the network during training so it learns to retrieve the right facts.

cards.title: How memory networks performed
body: Memory networks were tested on large-scale QA and toy reasoning tasks, showing big gains over previous models.
They excelled at chaining facts together, especially for questions that require multi-step reasoning or context.
cards.caption: Memory networks set new benchmarks for QA tasks that require reasoning over stored information.

cards.title: Why Memory Networks changed AI
body: This paper showed that separating memory from reasoning lets neural networks answer questions more like humans do.
It opened up new ways for AI to use external knowledge, influencing later work like transformers and retrieval-augmented models.

recap.label: Store
recap.note: Save facts in external memory slots.

recap.label: Retrieve
recap.note: Find relevant facts for each question.

recap.label: Chain
recap.note: Combine multiple facts to reason through complex queries.

recap.label: Answer
recap.note: Produce an informed response using retrieved information.

cards.title: Memory Networks
body: Read the original paper to explore the details behind this influential model.
